title: Adult Viral URI - Sore Throat and Dry Cough
chiefLine: Sore throat + dry cough x4 days, low-grade fever

patient.name: Daniel Okafor
patient.reason: Sore throat and dry cough for 4 days with low-grade fever

steps.stem: You call the patient back to the exam room. Before starting the visit, how should you verify you have the right patient?
options: Confirm his full name and date of birth
Confirm the room number and the appointment time
Ask 'Are you Daniel?' and proceed when he nods
Match the name on the chart to the name on the door
steps.explain: Always use TWO patient identifiers. Full name plus date of birth is the standard acceptable pair. Room number, appointment time, and a simple yes/nod are NOT acceptable identifiers and can lead to wrong-patient errors.

Adult normals: Temp 97.0-99.5 F, Pulse 60-100, Resp 12-20, BP 90-120/60-80, SpO2 95-100%. Everything here is normal EXCEPT the temperature of 100.6 F, which meets the fever threshold (>=100.4 F). Document it accurately and report it to the provider. A low-grade fever with sore throat and cough fits a self-limiting viral picture, but the MA only measures and documents - the provider interprets it.

steps.stem: Which entry correctly documents the chief complaint in the Subjective section?
options: 'Sore throat and dry cough for 4 days' in the patient's own words
'Viral upper respiratory infection'
'Pharyngeal erythema, no exudate'
'Give supportive care and fluids'
steps.explain: The chief complaint is what the patient reports, ideally in their own words, and belongs in Subjective. A diagnosis (viral URI) is the provider's Assessment, exam findings are Objective, and treatment is the Plan.

steps.stem: The CLIA-waived rapid strep test you performed comes back negative. What is your correct action as the MA?
options: Document the negative result and report it to the provider
Tell the patient it is viral and he does not need antibiotics
Enter 'viral URI' as the diagnosis in the chart
Start the patient on acetaminophen on your own
steps.explain: The MA performs the POC test and documents the objective result, then reports it to the provider. Interpreting the result diagnostically, diagnosing, and starting treatment are all provider functions outside MA scope.

steps.intro: Tag each item from this visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: Reports sore throat and dry cough for 4 days
States he has felt feverish at home
Temperature 100.6 F
Rapid strep test negative
Pharyngeal erythema without exudate
Viral upper respiratory infection
Advise rest, fluids, and acetaminophen for fever
Return if symptoms worsen or fever persists

steps.stem: The patient describes a dry cough with no phlegm. Which term correctly documents this?
options: Nonproductive cough (no sputum produced)
Productive cough with purulent sputum
Hemoptysis
Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea
steps.explain: A dry cough that brings up no phlegm is a nonproductive cough. Productive means sputum is raised, hemoptysis is coughing up blood, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea is sudden nighttime shortness of breath - none of which apply here.

S: CC: 'My throat has been sore and I've had a dry cough for 4 days.'
33-year-old male, sore throat and nonproductive cough x4 days
Reports subjective low-grade fever at home; denies shortness of breath or chest pain
No known sick contacts reported; no difficulty swallowing
PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: NKDA
O: Temp 100.6 F (fever), Pulse 82, Resp 16, BP 118/76, SpO2 98% on room air
Pain 3/10 (throat). Weight 180 lb, Height 70 in
Rapid strep test: negative
Provider exam: pharyngeal erythema without exudate, no cervical lymphadenopathy, lungs clear
P: Supportive care: rest, increased fluids
Acetaminophen for fever and throat discomfort per label
Throat lozenges as needed
No antibiotics indicated (strep negative, viral picture)
Return if symptoms worsen or fever persists beyond 3 days

teaching: Verify identity with two identifiers - full name + date of birth - never room number or appointment time.
A temperature of 100.4 F or higher is a fever; 100.6 F is the one abnormal vital here and must be documented and reported.
The MA performs and documents POC tests like rapid strep, but interpreting, diagnosing, and prescribing are provider functions.
Chief complaint and reported symptoms go in Subjective; vitals, exam findings, and test results go in Objective.
scopeNote: The MA gathers the history and vitals and documents the negative strep result; the provider makes the viral URI diagnosis and orders supportive care.

title: Hypertension 3-Month Follow-Up & Refill

patient.name: Diane Whitfield
patient.reason: 3-month blood pressure check and medication refill

steps.stem: Before rooming Ms. Whitfield, how do you correctly verify her identity?
options: Confirm she is in exam room 4 at her 10:00 appointment
Ask her to state her full name and date of birth, then match to the chart
Call her name in the waiting room and confirm she answers
Confirm the name printed on the encounter form is correct
steps.explain: Always use TWO patient identifiers. The accepted standard pair is full name + date of birth, matched against the chart/wristband. Room number, appointment time, or the patient simply answering to a called name are NOT acceptable identifiers.

Adult BP normal is <120/<80; 130/80 or higher flags as hypertension. Ms. Whitfield's 158/96 is clearly elevated and is the key finding today. Best practice: patient seated 5 min, feet flat, correct cuff size, arm supported at heart level, then recheck after a few minutes and document both readings for the provider. Temp, pulse, resp, and SpO2 are all within normal adult ranges.

steps.stem: Which entry best documents the chief complaint for this visit?
options: Patient likely has uncontrolled hypertension needing a dose increase
Hypertension — start amlodipine and recheck in 2 weeks
"I'm here for my blood pressure check and to refill my pills."
Elevated BP secondary to medication nonadherence
steps.explain: The chief complaint is recorded in the patient's own words and states why they came. Options implying a diagnosis or a treatment plan are provider functions (Assessment/Plan), not the MA-documented chief complaint.

steps.stem: During medication reconciliation, Ms. Whitfield says she stopped her hydrochlorothiazide two weeks ago because it made her urinate too much. What is the correct MA action?
options: Tell her to restart the hydrochlorothiazide today at the same dose
Reassure her the water pill isn't important and can be skipped
Document that she stopped hydrochlorothiazide, the reason, and flag it for the provider
Diagnose her elevated BP as caused by stopping the diuretic
steps.explain: The MA gathers and documents an accurate medication list — including what the patient actually takes, stopped, or changed, and why — then flags discrepancies for the provider. Restarting, adjusting, or diagnosing are provider decisions outside MA scope.

steps.stem: The provider hasn't come in yet, and Ms. Whitfield asks, "Do you think my doctor will raise my blood pressure medicine?" What is the appropriate MA response?
options: "Yes, at 158/96 you'll definitely need a higher dose."
"I can't say — the provider will review your readings and decide. I'll make sure they see today's numbers."
"Probably not, your pressure looks fine to me."
"I'll go ahead and put in the refill at a higher dose for you."
steps.explain: Interpreting results diagnostically, predicting treatment, prescribing, or ordering medication changes are provider functions. The MA documents findings, reassures appropriately, and defers clinical decisions to the provider.

steps.intro: Sort each item from today's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: "I'm here for my blood pressure check and refill."
Stopped hydrochlorothiazide 2 weeks ago due to frequent urination
BP 158/96 mmHg, recheck 156/94 mmHg
Weight 182 lb, height 64 in
Uncontrolled essential hypertension
Continue lisinopril; resume hydrochlorothiazide and counsel on adherence
Recheck BP in 2 weeks; provide low-sodium diet education

steps.stem: The provider asks you to document the medication refill order. Which entry follows safe abbreviation standards?
options: Lisinopril 20.0 mg PO QD
Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
Lisinopril 20 mg PO qd
Lisinopril 20mg by mouth QD
steps.explain: Avoid TJC 'Do Not Use' items: 'QD/qd' can be misread as 'QID' or 'OD' — spell out 'daily.' Never use a trailing zero (20.0 mg can be misread as 200 mg). '20 mg PO daily' is clear and safe.

S: Chief complaint: "I'm here for my blood pressure check and to refill my pills."
58-year-old female presenting for 3-month hypertension follow-up and medication refill
Reports stopping hydrochlorothiazide ~2 weeks ago due to bothersome frequent urination
Denies chest pain, headache, dizziness, or visual changes
PMH: essential hypertension, hyperlipidemia, overweight
Allergy: sulfa (sulfonamides)
O: BP 158/96 mmHg (elevated), recheck 156/94 mmHg
Temp 98.4 F, pulse 78 bpm, resp 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air
Weight 182 lb, height 64 in
Pain 0/10
Current meds documented: lisinopril 20 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily (self-discontinued), atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily
A: Elevated BP in setting of self-discontinued diuretic (provider impression)
P: Continue lisinopril 20 mg PO daily; refilled
Resume hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg PO daily; counsel on adherence and timing
Low-sodium (DASH) diet education provided
Recheck BP in 2 weeks
Return sooner for chest pain, severe headache, or visual changes

teaching: Two identifiers = full name + DOB; room number and appointment time never count.
BP of 130/80 or higher flags as hypertension; 158/96 is clearly elevated — position the patient correctly and recheck before finalizing.
Medication reconciliation means documenting what the patient actually takes (including stopped/changed meds and why) and flagging discrepancies — never adjusting doses.
Assessment and Plan are provider functions; the MA documents them as dictated but does not diagnose, predict, or prescribe.
scopeNote: The MA verifies ID, obtains vitals, and documents the history, med reconciliation, and the provider's A/P — but does not diagnose, adjust doses, or authorize the refill.

title: Type 2 Diabetes 3-Month Follow-Up

patient.name: Raymond Alvarez
meds: Metformin 1000 mg by mouth twice daily

steps.stem: You call Raymond back for his diabetes follow-up. Before starting, how do you correctly verify his identity?
options: Confirm he answered to the name you called in the waiting room
Ask him to state his full name and date of birth, then match both to the chart
Verify his room number and appointment time
Ask, 'You're Raymond Alvarez, right?' and proceed when he says yes
steps.explain: Use TWO patient identifiers before any care. The accepted standard pair is full name plus date of birth, matched to the chart and armband/record. Room number, appointment time, and a yes/no confirmation of a name you supplied are NOT acceptable identifiers and can lead to wrong-patient errors.

Adult ranges: Temp 97.0-99.5 F, Pulse 60-100, Resp 12-20, BP under 130/80, SpO2 95-100%. Raymond's vitals and BP (128/78) are within range. The flag is the POC random glucose of 187 mg/dL - a random glucose at or above 140 is elevated and above his target. Document the value and report it to the provider; do not interpret it as a diagnosis or adjust medications yourself.

steps.stem: Raymond says, 'My sugars have been running high in the mornings and my feet feel kind of numb and tingly.' How should you document the chief complaint?
options: Patient has diabetic peripheral neuropathy and poor glycemic control
Elevated morning blood glucose; needs medication increase
'My sugars have been running high in the mornings and my feet feel kind of numb and tingly'
Patient noncompliant with diabetes management
steps.explain: The chief complaint is recorded in the patient's own words as reported. Options that assign a diagnosis (neuropathy), recommend a treatment, or label the patient are outside MA scope and add unverified clinical interpretation. Quote the subjective report; let the provider diagnose.

steps.stem: The provider asks you to perform a monofilament diabetic foot exam and document findings, then plans to order an A1c. Which action is within your MA scope?
options: Tell Raymond his foot numbness means he has nerve damage from diabetes
Perform the delegated monofilament screening and document the sensation findings you observe
Increase his metformin dose because the glucose is high
Independently order the A1c lab before the provider sees him
steps.explain: MAs carry out delegated screenings (like the monofilament foot exam) and document objective findings. They do NOT diagnose (nerve damage), prescribe or adjust medications, or originate lab orders. Diagnosis and orders are provider functions the MA may document once the provider states them.

steps.intro: Tag each statement from Raymond's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: Reports morning blood sugars running high
States feet feel numb and tingly
POC random glucose 187 mg/dL
BP 128/78, weight 214 lb
Monofilament: reduced sensation both great toes
Type 2 diabetes, suboptimal control
Order hemoglobin A1c via venipuncture
Return to clinic in 2 weeks to review labs

steps.stem: The provider dictates the plan for you to document. Which entry avoids TJC 'Do Not Use' abbreviations and is written correctly?
options: Metformin 1000 mg by mouth twice daily; A1c ordered; foot care education given
Metformin 1.0 g PO QD; A1c ordered; foot care education given
Metformin 1000mg by mouth QD; recheck sugar QD
Metformin 1.0 gram by mouth two times a day; draw A1c
steps.explain: Spell out 'daily'/'twice daily' instead of QD or BID hazards, avoid trailing zeros (1.0 mg is prohibited - a missed decimal reads as 10x), and keep clear units. 'Metformin 1000 mg by mouth twice daily' uses no Do Not Use abbreviations and no trailing zero, so it is the safe, correct entry.

S: 62-year-old male here for scheduled 3-month type 2 diabetes follow-up
Reports morning blood sugars 'running high'
Reports bilateral foot numbness and tingling
Denies chest pain, foot wounds, or vision changes
Reports adherence to metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin
O: Temp 98.2 F, Pulse 78, Resp 16, BP 128/78, SpO2 98% on room air
Weight 214 lb, Height 70 in
POC random glucose 187 mg/dL (elevated)
Monofilament foot exam: reduced sensation at both great toes; skin intact, no ulcers
Pain 0/10
A: Type 2 diabetes mellitus with suboptimal glycemic control (provider)
Suspected diabetic peripheral neuropathy, to be evaluated (provider)
P: Hemoglobin A1c ordered via venipuncture (provider)
Continue current metformin, lisinopril, atorvastatin pending A1c (provider)
Diabetic foot care and daily self-inspection education provided
Return to clinic in 2 weeks to review A1c results (provider)

teaching: Two identifiers before care: full name + date of birth. Room number, appointment time, and 'the patient said yes' never count.
A random glucose at or above 140 mg/dL is elevated - the MA documents and reports the value but never diagnoses or changes meds.
The MA gathers/documents S and O; the provider originates A and P. The MA may only document diagnoses and orders the provider dictates.
Avoid Do Not Use abbreviations: spell out 'daily', use 'mL' not cc, and never use trailing zeros (1.0 mg) which cause 10x dosing errors.
scopeNote: The MA collects and documents the subjective history, vitals, POC glucose, and delegated foot screening; the provider originates the diabetes assessment, the A1c order, and the plan.

title: 4-Year-Old Well-Child Check

patient.name: Mateo Reyes
meds: Children's multivitamin, 1 gummy by mouth daily
pmh: Up to date on immunizations through 18 months
patient.reason: Routine 4-year well-child check; kindergarten physical and vaccines due

steps.stem: You call Mateo back for his well-child check. His mother is with him. What is the correct way to verify his identity before beginning?
options: Confirm he is the child in room 3 scheduled for 9:00 AM
Ask the mother to state the child's full name and date of birth, and match both to the chart
Ask the child if his name is Mateo and proceed when he nods
Verify the mother's name and assume the child matches
steps.explain: Always use TWO patient identifiers. For a young child, have the parent/guardian state the child's full name AND date of birth, then match both against the chart and armband/record. Room number, appointment time, and a child nodding to a leading question are NOT acceptable identifiers.

steps.stem: Obtain and document Mateo's vital signs and growth measurements for the well-child visit.
steps.explain: Use PRESCHOOL ranges, which differ from adult ranges: pulse 80-120 bpm (his 96 is normal), respirations 20-30/min (his 24 is normal), systolic BP roughly 90-110 (his 100/62 is normal), SpO2 95-100% (his 99% is normal), temp 97.0-99.5 F (his 98.4 is normal). A pulse of 96 would be borderline-high for an adult but is perfectly normal for a 4-year-old — this is why age-specific ranges matter. Height and weight are plotted on a growth chart to track percentiles over time.

steps.stem: How should the MA document the reason for today's visit in the chief complaint?
options: Patient is healthy, no issues
Mother reports here for routine 4-year well-child check and kindergarten physical; vaccines due
Well child, diagnosis deferred to provider
Annual exam, R/O developmental delay
steps.explain: The chief complaint captures why the patient is here, as reported by the patient or guardian, in clear objective language. A well-child visit is documented as a routine/preventive check with the specific purpose (kindergarten physical, immunizations due). Don't add rule-out diagnoses or clinical impressions — that is the provider's job.

steps.stem: The provider says Mateo is due for his DTaP, IPV, MMR, and varicella boosters and asks you to administer them per protocol. The mother asks you, 'Does my son really need all four shots today?' What is the appropriate MA response?
options: Reassure her they are optional and she can skip any she wants
Explain your clinical opinion on which vaccines matter most
Let her know you'll bring the provider back to discuss her questions, then administer the ordered vaccines once she consents
Tell her the shots are required by law so she has no choice
steps.explain: The MA administers vaccines as a delegated order and provides standard education (like the VIS sheets), but deciding which vaccines are needed and counseling on medical necessity is the provider's role. Bring clinical questions back to the provider. The MA does not diagnose, prescribe, or override informed consent.

steps.intro: Sort each item from Mateo's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: Mother reports Mateo is eating well, sleeping through the night, and speaking in full sentences
No parental concerns about vision, hearing, or behavior
Weight 37 lb, height 40 in, plotted at ~50th percentile on growth chart
Vitals: temp 98.4 F, pulse 96, resp 24, BP 100/62, SpO2 99%
Healthy 4-year-old, growth and development age-appropriate
Administer DTaP, IPV, MMR, and varicella boosters today
Return for next well-child visit at age 5

steps.stem: When documenting the multivitamin the child takes, which entry avoids TJC 'Do Not Use' abbreviations and is correct?
options: Children's multivitamin 1 gummy PO QD
Children's multivitamin, 1 gummy by mouth daily
Children's multivitamin 1.0 gummy q.d.
Children's multivitamin 1 gummy PO qds
steps.explain: Spell out 'daily' instead of QD/q.d. (mistaken for QID or OD), spell out 'by mouth' or use 'PO' cautiously, and never use a trailing zero (1.0). 'By mouth daily' is unambiguous and compliant with the TJC Do Not Use list.

S: Mother reports Mateo here for routine 4-year well-child check and kindergarten physical
Eating well, sleeping through the night, speaking in full sentences
No parental concerns about vision, hearing, or behavior
Allergies: NKDA. Med: children's multivitamin, 1 gummy by mouth daily
Up to date on immunizations through 18 months
O: Temp 98.4 F, pulse 96, resp 24, BP 100/62, SpO2 99% on room air (all age-normal for preschool)
Weight 37 lb, height 40 in, plotted ~50th percentile on growth chart
Alert, active, interactive, no acute distress
A: Healthy 4-year-old male; growth and development age-appropriate (provider assessment)
P: Administer DTaP, IPV, MMR, and varicella boosters today per provider order
Anticipatory guidance provided to mother; VIS sheets given
Return for next well-child visit at age 5 or sooner if concerns

teaching: Pediatric vital ranges differ from adult ranges — a pulse of 96 and respirations of 24 are normal for a 4-year-old but would be flagged in an adult. Always use age-specific ranges.
Use two identifiers even for children: have the parent state the child's full name and date of birth; room number and appointment time never count.
The MA administers vaccines as a delegated order and gives standard VIS education, but the provider decides which vaccines are needed and counsels on medical necessity.
Avoid TJC Do Not Use abbreviations: write 'daily' not QD, no trailing zeros (1 mg not 1.0 mg).
scopeNote: MA gathers/documents S and O (vitals, growth, history) and administers ordered vaccines; the provider owns the well-child assessment and immunization plan.

title: Acute Chest Pressure in Urgent Care
chiefLine: 55M — chest pressure and sweating, sudden onset

patient.name: Robert Alvarez
patient.reason: Sudden chest pressure with sweating for the past 40 minutes

steps.stem: Mr. Alvarez is brought back looking pale and diaphoretic. Before you place him on the monitor, how do you correctly verify his identity?
options: Confirm his full name and date of birth
Confirm the appointment time on the schedule
Ask "Are you Robert?" and proceed when he nods
Match him to the room number on the door
steps.explain: Always use TWO patient identifiers, and the standard acceptable pair is full name + date of birth. Room number, appointment time, and a simple yes/no to a name are NOT acceptable identifiers. Emergencies do not waive identity verification — you still confirm name + DOB before the EKG and any intervention.

Adult normals: Pulse 60-100 (his 108 = tachycardia), Resp 12-20 (his 22 = tachypnea), BP <120/80 (his 158/94 = hypertension flag), SpO2 95-100% (his 93% = hypoxemia). Pain 7/10 is significant. This cluster — tachycardia, tachypnea, hypertension, low SpO2, diaphoresis, and chest pressure in a 55-year-old with cardiac risk factors — is an emergency pattern. Get the provider immediately and be ready to activate EMS; the MA does not wait to 're-check' before escalating.

steps.stem: Which entry BEST documents his chief complaint in the Subjective section?
options: "Patient states: chest pressure like a weight, started 40 minutes ago, sweating"
"Rule out myocardial infarction"
"Chest pain, likely cardiac in origin"
"Elevated troponin suspected"
steps.explain: The chief complaint is the patient's own reported experience — onset, quality, timing — recorded objectively without interpretation. 'Rule out MI' and 'likely cardiac' are clinical impressions (Assessment), which the MA does not originate. Document what the patient says and what you measure, not a diagnosis.

steps.stem: The provider asks you to note the EKG order. Which is written correctly, avoiding "Do Not Use" abbreviations?
options: 12-lead EKG obtained; aspirin 81 mg daily documented on home med list
EKG obtained; ASA 1.0 mg QD per home meds
EKG done; give ASA cc dose stat
EKG obtained; aspirin .81 mg QOD
steps.explain: Spell out 'daily' (not QD), never use a trailing zero (1.0 mg is unsafe — reads as 10), always use a leading zero for decimals (0.81, never .81), and don't abbreviate drug names ambiguously. 'cc' is a Do Not Use unit; use mL. Clean, unambiguous documentation prevents medication errors.

steps.stem: You've obtained the 12-lead EKG and vitals. The tracing looks abnormal to you and the patient's pressure is worsening. What is the correct MA action?
options: Hand the EKG to the provider immediately and report the abnormal vitals so they can evaluate — escalate, do not diagnose
Tell the patient he is having a heart attack and reassure him
Interpret the EKG as a STEMI and chart that diagnosis
Give him a nitroglycerin tablet from the med room to help the pain
steps.explain: The MA recognizes an emergency and escalates immediately — get the EKG and vitals to the provider without delay. The MA does NOT interpret the EKG diagnostically, does NOT tell the patient the diagnosis, and does NOT give medication without a provider order. Diagnosis (A) and treatment/prescriptions (P) are provider functions. Recognizing and escalating is exactly the MA's job here.

steps.intro: Sort each item from Mr. Alvarez's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: "Chest pressure like a weight, started 40 minutes ago"
History of hypertension and high cholesterol
BP 158/94, pulse 108, SpO2 93% on room air
12-lead EKG obtained and given to provider
Patient appears pale and diaphoretic
Provider impression: acute coronary syndrome
Activate EMS for transfer to emergency department
Administer aspirin per provider order

steps.stem: While the provider evaluates and EMS is activated, what is the MA's appropriate role?
options: Stay with the patient, monitor and re-check vitals, and carry out delegated orders like placing him on oxygen when ordered
Leave to start the next patient's intake since the provider is now in charge
Independently decide to send him home with follow-up instructions
Call the patient's family to explain his cardiac diagnosis
steps.explain: The MA supports the emergency: stay with the patient, keep monitoring vitals, and perform delegated tasks (oxygen, repeat EKG, IV setup per protocol) as ordered. The MA does not disposition the patient, does not diagnose to family, and doesn't abandon an unstable patient. Supporting and documenting under provider direction is in scope.

S: Chief complaint: "chest pressure like a weight" starting 40 minutes ago, associated with sweating
55-year-old male; pain rated 7/10, pressure-like, substernal
PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, former smoker (quit 3 years ago)
Home meds: lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily
Allergies: NKDA
O: Vitals: Temp 98.4 F, pulse 108, resp 22, BP 158/94, SpO2 93% on room air, pain 7/10
Appears pale and diaphoretic
12-lead EKG obtained and provided to provider immediately
Abnormal findings flagged: tachycardia, tachypnea, hypertension, hypoxemia
A: Provider clinical impression: acute coronary syndrome, rule out myocardial infarction (documented as dictated by provider)
P: EMS activated for emergent transfer to emergency department
Aspirin administered per provider order
Oxygen applied per provider order; continuous monitoring
MA to stay with patient, repeat vitals, and carry out delegated orders until transfer

teaching: Two identifiers (full name + DOB) are required every time — emergencies do not waive identity verification.
The MA recognizes and escalates emergencies but never diagnoses; interpreting an EKG or naming an MI is a provider function.
Chief complaint is documented in the patient's own words plus objective measurements — never as a diagnosis.
Avoid TJC 'Do Not Use' items: spell out 'daily', use leading zeros (0.81), no trailing zeros (1.0), use mL not cc.
scopeNote: The MA gathers vitals and the EKG and escalates immediately to the provider; diagnosis and treatment orders remain provider functions the MA only documents and carries out.

title: Asthma Exacerbation with Wheeze and Shortness of Breath
chiefLine: Wheezing and shortness of breath since this morning

patient.name: Maya Delgado
meds: Albuterol HFA inhaler 90 mcg, 2 puffs every 4 hours as needed
Fluticasone 110 mcg inhaler, 1 puff twice daily
patient.reason: Wheezing, chest tightness, and shortness of breath worsening over the day

steps.stem: Maya arrives in respiratory distress and is anxious. Before obtaining vitals, how do you correctly verify her identity?
options: Confirm her full name and date of birth against the chart
Confirm she is in room 3 and it is her 2:15 appointment
Ask 'Are you Maya?' and proceed when she nods
Verify her full name and the reason for today's visit
steps.explain: Use TWO acceptable identifiers: full name plus date of birth is the standard pair. Room number, appointment time, and a yes/no name check are NOT acceptable identifiers. Distress does not waive verification.

Adult ranges: Pulse 60-100, Resp 12-20, BP sys 90-120/dia 60-80, SpO2 95-100%. Maya's SpO2 91% is hypoxemia (below 95%) and is the priority abnormal finding — report it to the provider immediately. Resp 24 is tachypnea and pulse 104 is mild tachycardia, both consistent with respiratory distress. Peak flow 250 L/min is well below her personal best (~450), showing significant airway obstruction. BP 126/78 is elevated but not hypertensive.

steps.stem: How should you document Maya's chief complaint in the Subjective section?
options: Patient states, 'I can't catch my breath and my chest feels tight,' since this morning
Acute asthma exacerbation with hypoxemia
Bronchospasm secondary to viral trigger
Patient appears short of breath and is wheezing on exam
steps.explain: The chief complaint is recorded in the patient's own words as Subjective data. Naming a diagnosis (asthma exacerbation, bronchospasm) is the provider's Assessment, and observed wheezing is Objective — neither belongs in the CC.

steps.stem: With an SpO2 of 91% and audible wheezing, which action is within your MA scope for this visit?
options: Notify the provider of the low SpO2 and distress immediately, and document findings
Tell Maya she is having a moderate asthma attack
Start an oral steroid and increase her inhaler dose
Interpret the peak flow as diagnostic of an exacerbation
steps.explain: The MA recognizes the emergency and escalates the abnormal SpO2 to the provider right away, then documents. Diagnosing the attack, interpreting results diagnostically, and starting or changing medications are provider functions the MA never originates.

steps.intro: Tag each item from Maya's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: States 'I can't catch my breath and my chest feels tight'
Reports using rescue inhaler twice with little relief
SpO2 91% on room air
Expiratory wheezing heard bilaterally
Peak flow 250 L/min, reduced from personal best
Acute moderate asthma exacerbation
Administer nebulized albuterol treatment per provider order
Educate patient on proper inhaler and spacer technique

steps.stem: The provider orders a nebulized bronchodilator. Which documentation entry uses correct, approved terminology?
options: Give 3 mL albuterol nebulized now, then recheck SpO2 and peak flow
Give 3 cc albuterol nebulized now
Give albuterol neb QD
Give 3.0 mL albuterol nebulized now
steps.explain: Use 'mL', not 'cc'. Avoid 'QD' (write 'daily' or the actual frequency) and never use a trailing zero like '3.0 mL', which can be misread as 30 mL. Rechecking SpO2 and peak flow after the treatment documents the response to therapy.

S: Patient states, 'I can't catch my breath and my chest feels tight,' since this morning
Wheezing and shortness of breath worsening over the day, worse with exertion
Used albuterol rescue inhaler twice at home with little relief
Recent upper respiratory infection last week; denies fever or chest pain
History of mild persistent asthma and seasonal allergies
O: Temp 98.7 F, Pulse 104, Resp 24, BP 126/78, SpO2 91% on room air
Peak flow 250 L/min, reduced from personal best ~450 L/min
Audible bilateral expiratory wheezing with mild accessory muscle use
Speaking in short sentences, mild respiratory distress
A: Acute moderate asthma exacerbation with hypoxemia (provider assessment)
P: Nebulized albuterol treatment administered per provider order
Recheck SpO2 and peak flow after treatment
Oral corticosteroid started per provider
Reviewed inhaler and spacer technique with patient
Follow up in 24-48 hours; return precautions given for worsening breathing

teaching: Chief complaint goes in the patient's own words as Subjective data; the diagnosis is the provider's Assessment.
SpO2 below 95% is hypoxemia — recognize it, escalate to the provider immediately, and document; the MA never diagnoses or prescribes.
Peak flow and SpO2 are Objective measurements; comparing peak flow to the patient's personal best shows severity.
Chart with approved terms: use 'mL' not 'cc', 'daily' not 'QD', and no trailing zeros (3 mL, not 3.0 mL).
scopeNote: The MA gathers and documents S and O and escalates the low SpO2; the provider originates the asthma diagnosis (A) and all treatment orders (P).

title: Dysuria and Urinary Frequency — Clean-Catch Urinalysis
chiefLine: Burning with urination and going frequently x2 days

patient.name: Amara Ellison
meds: Ethinyl estradiol/norethindrone oral contraceptive, one tablet daily
patient.reason: Burning with urination and urinary frequency for 2 days

steps.stem: You call Ms. Ellison back to the room. Before starting, how do you correctly verify her identity?
options: Ask her to state her full name and date of birth, then match both to the chart
Confirm she is the patient in room 4 at the 2:00 PM slot
Ask 'Are you Amara Ellison?' and proceed when she says yes
Match her first name and phone number to the schedule
steps.explain: Use two identifiers that belong to the person, not the location. Full name plus date of birth is the standard acceptable pair. Room number, appointment time, or a single yes/no confirmation are NOT acceptable identifiers because they can be shared or misheard.

Every reading is within adult normal ranges: Temp 97.0-99.5 F (98.6 is normal, no fever; fever is >=100.4), Pulse 60-100 (78), Resp 12-20 (16), BP systolic 90-120 / diastolic 60-80 (118/74 normal), SpO2 95-100% (99%). Pain of 3/10 is documented as present (any score >=1 counts). The lack of fever matters here: an afebrile patient with lower-urinary symptoms fits a simple bladder picture rather than a kidney infection, but only the provider assigns that impression.

steps.stem: Ms. Ellison says, 'It burns so bad when I pee, and I keep running to the bathroom.' How should you document the chief complaint?
options: Chief complaint: 'It burns so bad when I pee, and I keep running to the bathroom' x2 days
Assessment: acute urinary tract infection
Patient has dysuria secondary to cystitis
Rule out UTI, start antibiotics
steps.explain: The chief complaint is recorded in the patient's own words. Naming a diagnosis (UTI, cystitis) or ordering treatment is a provider function and is outside MA scope. 'Dysuria' and 'urinary frequency' are correct terms for the HPI, but the CC itself should quote the patient.

steps.stem: You will collect a clean-catch midstream urine specimen. Which instruction set is correct to teach the patient?
options: Wash hands, cleanse front to back with the wipes, begin voiding into the toilet, then catch the midstream urine in the cup
Collect the very first urine that comes out to get the most concentrated sample
Skip cleansing since the sample goes to a lab anyway
Wipe back to front, then fill the cup from the start of the stream
steps.explain: Clean-catch midstream technique reduces contamination: hand hygiene, cleanse the meatus front to back (away from the rectum), start the stream into the toilet, then collect the midstream portion. First-stream urine and back-to-front wiping both increase contamination and can produce a misleading result.

steps.stem: The CLIA-waived urine dipstick shows positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites. What is the correct MA action?
options: Document the dipstick results and report them to the provider, who interprets them and decides on treatment
Tell the patient she has a UTI and it should clear up soon
Start the patient on an antibiotic from the sample cabinet
Interpret the result as a confirmed infection in the note
steps.explain: The MA performs the CLIA-waived test and documents the objective result, then reports it to the provider. Interpreting the result as a diagnosis, telling the patient what it means clinically, or starting a medication are provider functions outside MA scope.

steps.intro: Tag each item from this visit into Subjective, Objective, Assessment, or Plan.

items.text: 'It burns so bad when I pee' for 2 days
Reports urinary frequency and no fever or flank pain
Temp 98.6 F, BP 118/74, pain 3/10
Dipstick positive for leukocyte esterase and nitrites
Clinical impression: acute uncomplicated cystitis
Prescribe nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for 5 days
Educate on hydration and completing the antibiotic course
Return if fever, back pain, or symptoms worsen

steps.stem: The provider dictates the plan. Which entry is written correctly per Joint Commission 'Do Not Use' guidance?
options: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days
Nitrofurantoin 100.0 mg PO QD
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO QID x5d, 1.0 tab
Push fluids, 1.0 L daily, take antibiotic QD
steps.explain: Spell out frequency ('twice daily,' not QD which can be misread as QID) and avoid trailing zeros (100 mg, not 100.0 mg, which can look like 1000 mg). 'By mouth' is safer than assuming abbreviations. Clear, unambiguous entries prevent medication errors.

S: Chief complaint: 'It burns so bad when I pee, and I keep running to the bathroom' x2 days
26-year-old female reports dysuria and urinary frequency for 2 days
Denies fever, chills, flank/back pain, nausea, or vaginal discharge
No known drug allergies; on oral contraceptive and as-needed cetirizine
PMH: seasonal allergic rhinitis
O: Vitals: Temp 98.6 F, Pulse 78, Resp 16, BP 118/74, SpO2 99% on room air
Pain 3/10 with voiding; afebrile
Height 65 in, weight 141 lb
Clean-catch midstream urine dipstick: positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites
P: (Provider) Nitrofurantoin 100 mg by mouth twice daily for 5 days
Educate on increasing fluid intake and completing the full antibiotic course
Return precautions: fever, flank/back pain, vomiting, or worsening symptoms
Follow up if symptoms not improved in 48-72 hours

teaching: Chief complaint is recorded in the patient's own words; the MA never assigns the diagnosis (Assessment) or the treatment (Plan).
Verify identity with two person-specific identifiers — full name plus date of birth. Room number and appointment time do not count.
Clean-catch midstream technique: hand hygiene, cleanse front to back, start stream into the toilet, catch the midstream portion to limit contamination.
Document POC test results objectively and report to the provider for interpretation; avoid 'Do Not Use' abbreviations (spell out 'twice daily,' no trailing zeros).
scopeNote: The MA collects and documents the clean-catch specimen, dipstick result, and patient-reported symptoms; the provider owns the diagnosis and the prescription.

title: Forearm Laceration from Kitchen Knife
chiefLine: Cut on forearm from a kitchen knife, bleeding controlled

patient.name: Marcus Bell
meds: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for pain
patient.reason: Laceration to right forearm from kitchen knife

steps.stem: Before rooming Mr. Bell, how should the MA correctly verify his identity?
options: Confirm his full name and date of birth
Confirm the room number he was assigned
Ask if he is the patient scheduled for this time slot
Confirm his name and current address
steps.explain: Always use TWO patient identifiers, and full name plus date of birth is the standard acceptable pair. Room number, appointment time, or a simple yes/no confirmation are NOT acceptable identifiers because they can be mixed up between patients.

Adult ranges: Temp 97.0-99.5 F, Pulse 60-100, Resp 12-20, BP systolic 90-120/diastolic 60-80, SpO2 95-100%, Pain 0-10. Mr. Bell's vitals are within normal limits except an elevated BP of 128/78 (systolic 120-129 = elevated category) and a pain score of 4/10, which is expected with an acute laceration. The mildly elevated BP is common with pain and situational stress; document it and recheck as ordered. No emergency findings, but always report abnormal vitals to the provider.

steps.stem: Which entry BEST documents the chief complaint in the Subjective?
options: "Patient states he cut his right forearm on a kitchen knife about 1 hour ago while cooking."
"Laceration requires 4 sutures and a tetanus booster."
"3 cm clean-edged laceration, no active bleeding on exam."
"Rule out tendon involvement; provider to close wound."
steps.explain: The chief complaint and HPI capture what the patient reports in their own words, including mechanism and timing. Option A is subjective/patient-reported. Option C is an objective exam finding, and B and D are Plan/Assessment items the provider originates, not the chief complaint.

steps.stem: The provider asks the MA to prepare for wound closure. Which action is WITHIN the MA's scope?
options: Set up a sterile suture tray and supplies for the provider
Suture the laceration since it is a simple wound
Order a tetanus (Tdap) vaccine and prescribe an antibiotic
Tell the patient how many sutures the wound will need
steps.explain: The MA prepares the sterile field/suture tray, gathers supplies, and assists as delegated. The MA does NOT suture, order or prescribe medications, or tell the patient a treatment plan the provider has not yet stated. Suturing, ordering the vaccine, and prescribing are all provider functions.

steps.stem: While documenting, the MA should check and record which item most relevant to this laceration?
options: Date of the patient's last tetanus (Td/Tdap) vaccine
The patient's last eye exam date
The patient's cholesterol level
The patient's preferred pharmacy hours
steps.explain: For any break in the skin, tetanus immunization status is essential subjective/historical data. The MA gathers and documents the date of the last Td/Tdap so the provider can decide whether a booster is needed. The MA documents this history but does not decide or order the booster.

steps.intro: Tag each statement from Mr. Bell's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: Patient reports cutting his right forearm on a kitchen knife while cooking
Last tetanus vaccine was about 9 years ago
Pain rated 4 out of 10 at the wound site
3 cm linear laceration to the right volar forearm, edges approximated, no active bleeding
BP 128/78, pulse 88, SpO2 99% on room air
Uncomplicated laceration of the right forearm without tendon or nerve involvement
Provider to close wound with sutures under sterile technique
Administer Tdap booster per provider order and give wound-care instructions

steps.stem: When documenting the provider's suture-care instructions, which is written correctly (no TJC "Do Not Use" abbreviations)?
options: "Take ibuprofen 400 mg by mouth daily as needed for pain."
"Take ibuprofen 400 mg PO QD prn pain."
"Take ibuprofen 400.0 mg by mouth every day."
"Take ibuprofen 400 mg PO once daily, recheck wound in 1.0 week."
steps.explain: Spell out "daily" instead of QD, and avoid trailing zeros (400.0 mg or 1.0 week) which can be misread as ten times the dose. Option A uses the fully spelled-out, unambiguous form the CCMA exam expects.

S: 34-year-old male reports cutting his right forearm on a kitchen knife about 1 hour ago while cooking
Reports pain 4/10 at the wound; bleeding controlled at home with pressure
Last tetanus (Td/Tdap) vaccine approximately 9 years ago
Allergies: NKDA; Meds: ibuprofen as needed; PMH: seasonal allergic rhinitis
O: Vitals: Temp 98.4 F, Pulse 88, Resp 16, BP 128/78 (elevated), SpO2 99% on room air, Pain 4/10
3 cm linear laceration to the right volar forearm, edges approximated, no active bleeding, no visible foreign body
Distal circulation, sensation, and movement intact per documented provider exam
A: Uncomplicated 3 cm laceration of the right forearm without tendon or nerve involvement (provider)
P: Provider to close wound with sutures under sterile technique; MA to set up sterile tray and assist
Administer Tdap booster per provider order (last dose ~9 years ago)
Wound-care and dry-dressing instructions provided; ibuprofen 400 mg by mouth as needed for pain
Return precautions for signs of infection; suture removal follow-up in 10-14 days

teaching: Two identifiers (full name + DOB) are required before any patient care; room number or appointment time do not count.
The MA gathers and documents S and O (chief complaint, history, vitals, exam findings) but never originates the A or P; suturing, ordering, and prescribing are provider functions.
For any skin break, always check and document the last tetanus (Td/Tdap) date so the provider can decide on a booster.
Avoid TJC Do Not Use abbreviations: spell out "daily" (not QD), never use trailing zeros (400.0 mg), and use "mL" not cc.
scopeNote: The MA sets up the sterile suture tray, documents wound findings and tetanus history, and administers delegated vaccines, but does not suture, diagnose, order, or prescribe.

title: First Prenatal Visit — Initial OB Intake
chiefLine: New OB — first prenatal visit, ~6 weeks by LMP

patient.name: Daniela Reyes
meds: Prenatal vitamin with folic acid 800 mcg daily
pmh: G2 P1 (one prior term vaginal delivery)
patient.reason: First prenatal visit — positive home pregnancy test, missed period

steps.stem: You call Daniela back for her first prenatal visit. Before starting the intake, how do you correctly verify her identity?
options: Confirm she is in exam room 3 as listed on the schedule
Ask her to state her full name and date of birth, then match both to the chart
Ask "Are you Daniela?" and proceed when she says yes
Verify her appointment time matches the 9:00 AM slot
steps.explain: Two identifiers are required, and the standard acceptable pair is full name + date of birth matched to the chart. Room number, appointment time, and a simple yes/no to a name are NOT acceptable identifiers.

All readings are within adult normal ranges: Temp 97.0-99.5 F, Pulse 60-100, Resp 12-20, BP systolic 90-120 / diastolic 60-80, SpO2 95-100%. Baseline weight (148 lb) and height (64 in) give a BMI of about 25.4 to trend gestational weight gain, and the baseline BP is critical for later comparison to screen for gestational hypertension and preeclampsia. Document these accurately as the pregnancy reference point.

steps.stem: Daniela says, "My last period started June 1st and my home test was positive." Which is the BEST way to document the chief complaint?
options: Patient is 6 weeks pregnant and needs prenatal care
"My last period started June 1st and my home test was positive" — presents for initial prenatal visit
Rule out intrauterine pregnancy versus ectopic
Confirmed viable pregnancy at 6 weeks gestation
steps.explain: The chief complaint is recorded in the patient's own words plus the reason for the visit. The MA documents what the patient reports — not a diagnosis, gestational-age conclusion, or clinical interpretation, which are provider functions.

steps.stem: As part of OB intake you perform a CLIA-waived urine dipstick. What are you screening for and how should you handle the result?
options: Protein and glucose — document the values and give them to the provider to interpret
Protein and glucose — tell the patient she likely has gestational diabetes
Pregnancy hCG only — to confirm she is actually pregnant
Ketones — and start her on IV fluids per standing order
steps.explain: The baseline prenatal urine dip screens for protein (preeclampsia risk) and glucose (diabetes risk). The MA performs the CLIA-waived test and documents the result as Objective data, but does not interpret it diagnostically or diagnose — that is the provider's role.

steps.stem: Daniela asks, "When is my baby due?" You calculated an estimated due date from her LMP. What is the appropriate MA response?
options: Give her the exact date and confirm the pregnancy is definitely due then
Document the LMP and let her know the provider will review dating and confirm the estimated due date
Tell her to schedule an ultrasound herself to find out
Decline to record the LMP since dating is a provider task
steps.explain: The MA documents the LMP and may calculate/record an estimated due date as data, but confirming and communicating official pregnancy dating is a provider function. Record the objective information and defer the clinical confirmation to the provider.

steps.intro: Sort each item from Daniela's initial OB visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: "My last period started June 1st and my home test was positive"
Reports no cramping, bleeding, or nausea
BP 114/72, weight 148 lb, BMI ~25.4
Urine dipstick negative for protein and glucose
Intrauterine pregnancy, approximately 6 weeks by LMP
Order routine prenatal labs and dating ultrasound
Continue prenatal vitamin with folic acid daily
Return in 4 weeks for follow-up prenatal visit

steps.stem: You are documenting the provider's plan for Daniela's prenatal vitamin. Which entry avoids TJC "Do Not Use" abbreviations and is correct?
options: Prenatal vitamin 1.0 tab QD
Prenatal vitamin 1 tablet daily
Prenatal vitamin 1 tab QD
Prenatal vitamin q.d. with folic acid
steps.explain: Write "daily" instead of QD/q.d. (mistaken for QID or other doses) and never use a trailing zero (1.0 reads as 10 if the decimal is missed). "1 tablet daily" is clear and compliant.

S: Chief complaint: "My last period started June 1st and my home test was positive" — presents for initial prenatal visit
G2 P1, one prior term vaginal delivery
Denies cramping, vaginal bleeding, or significant nausea
Taking prenatal vitamin with folic acid 800 mcg daily; NKDA
O: Vitals: Temp 98.4 F, Pulse 78, Resp 16, BP 114/72, SpO2 99%
Weight 148 lb, Height 64 in, BMI ~25.4 (baseline)
Urine dipstick: negative for protein and glucose
Pain 0/10
A: Intrauterine pregnancy, approximately 6 weeks gestation by LMP (provider)
LMP June 1, 2026; estimated due date to be confirmed with dating ultrasound (provider)
P: Order routine initial prenatal labs (CBC, blood type/Rh, antibody screen, infectious disease panel, urine culture)
Schedule dating/viability ultrasound
Continue prenatal vitamin with folic acid 1 tablet daily
Prenatal education provided; return in 4 weeks for follow-up prenatal visit

teaching: Two-identifier verification uses full name + date of birth matched to the chart — never room number, appointment time, or a yes/no to a name.
Baseline OB vitals (BP and weight/BMI) are the reference point for screening later gestational hypertension, preeclampsia, and weight-gain concerns.
The MA performs the CLIA-waived urine dip and documents protein/glucose as Objective data but never interprets it diagnostically — dating, diagnosis, and orders are provider functions.
Avoid TJC Do Not Use abbreviations: write "daily" not QD, "units" not U, no trailing zeros (1 mg not 1.0 mg).
scopeNote: The MA gathers and documents the LMP, baseline vitals, and urine-dip results; the provider confirms dating, diagnoses the pregnancy, and orders labs and the plan.

title: Ground-Level Fall in an Older Adult
chiefLine: Fell at home yesterday, feels dizzy when standing up

patient.name: Margaret Ellison
meds: Zolpidem 5 mg at bedtime as needed
patient.reason: Follow-up after a ground-level fall at home

steps.stem: You bring Ms. Ellison back for her fall follow-up. How do you correctly verify her identity before starting?
options: Confirm she is the patient in exam room 3 with the 10:00 appointment
Ask her to state her full name and date of birth, and match both to the chart
Call out "Margaret?" and proceed once she answers yes
Verify her full name only, since her daughter completed the intake form
steps.explain: Two identifiers are required, and full name plus date of birth is the standard acceptable pair. Room number, appointment time, or a patient simply answering to a name are NOT acceptable identifiers. In an older adult who may have hearing loss or early cognitive change, actively matching name and DOB to the chart prevents a wrong-patient error.

steps.stem: The provider orders orthostatic (positional) vitals. You record a supine set after she rests 5 minutes, then repeat 1 minute after she stands. Enter this visit's supine readings and note the standing change.
steps.explain: Supine reading is 138/82 with pulse 74; the engine flags this as hypertension (systolic >=130 or diastolic >=80). Standing at 1 minute she dropped to 116/70 with pulse 92. Orthostatic hypotension is a systolic fall >=20 mmHg or a diastolic fall >=10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing - her 22 mmHg systolic drop meets criteria and matches her dizziness and fall. Proper technique: rest supine 5 minutes, then measure standing at 1 and 3 minutes, same arm, and be ready to steady her. Report the positional drop to the provider promptly, since it points to a likely cause of the fall and a possible medication contribution.

steps.stem: Which entry best documents the chief complaint in the patient's own words?
options: Orthostatic hypotension secondary to polypharmacy
"I got dizzy when I stood up from the couch and went down - now I'm scared to walk"
Mechanical fall, rule out syncope
Patient is a fall risk and needs medication adjustment
steps.explain: The chief complaint is the patient's reason for the visit in her own words, quoted. The other options are clinical interpretations, impressions, or plans - those belong to the provider's assessment and plan, not the MA-documented chief complaint.

steps.stem: After you report the orthostatic drop and the seven-medication list, the provider is still with another patient. Ms. Ellison asks, "Should I just stop my water pill and the blood pressure ones?" What is the correct MA response?
options: "Yes, stop the hydrochlorothiazide and lisinopril - they're dropping your pressure"
"Cut each of your blood pressure pills in half until you feel steadier"
"I can't advise changing your medicines, but I'll make sure the provider reviews them with you before you leave"
"Keep taking everything exactly the same; nothing needs to change"
steps.explain: Adjusting, stopping, or dosing medications is a provider function - the MA does not prescribe, change orders, or give medical advice about whether to stop a drug. The MA gathers and documents the medication list and the patient's question, then routes it to the provider. Reassuring her that nothing should change would also be giving clinical advice outside MA scope.

steps.intro: Sort each item from Ms. Ellison's visit into the correct SOAP section.

items.text: "I got dizzy standing up and fell; now I'm afraid to walk"
Reports no loss of consciousness and no head strike
Supine BP 138/82, standing BP 116/70 with pulse rising to 92
Small bruise noted over the right hip; steady only with wall support
Orthostatic hypotension, likely medication-related, with elevated fall risk
Hold the zolpidem and reassess the antihypertensive regimen
Refer to physical therapy for gait and balance and provide a home-safety handout
Order a basic metabolic panel and recheck orthostatic vitals at follow-up

steps.stem: You are charting the provider's plan to hold the bedtime sleep aid. Which entry avoids "Do Not Use" abbreviations and is documented correctly?
options: Hold zolpidem 5.0 mg QHS
Hold zolpidem 5 mg at bedtime
D/C zolpidem 5.0 mg qd
Hold zolpidem 5 mg QD
steps.explain: "5 mg at bedtime" is correct: no trailing zero (5.0 mg can be misread as 50 mg), and "at bedtime" is spelled out instead of the prohibited QHS. "QD" for daily and "D/C" are also on Do Not Use / error-prone lists. Sedative-hypnotics like zolpidem raise fall risk in older adults, so this medication is a logical one for the provider to hold.

S: Chief complaint: "I got dizzy when I stood up from the couch and went down - now I'm scared to walk."
78-year-old woman seen after a ground-level fall at home yesterday; felt lightheaded on standing before falling
Denies loss of consciousness, head strike, chest pain, palpitations, or new weakness; reports mild right hip soreness
Reports two near-falls in the past month, worse when getting up at night to use the bathroom
PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, knee osteoarthritis, osteopenia, insomnia. Allergy: sulfa
Home meds reviewed: lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, metoprolol tartrate, amlodipine, zolpidem as needed, atorvastatin, aspirin 81 mg
O: Supine: BP 138/82, pulse 74, resp 16, temp 98.2 F, SpO2 97% on room air
Standing at 1 minute: BP 116/70, pulse 92 (systolic drop of 22 mmHg)
Weight 149 lb, height 63 in, pain 3/10 at right hip, POC glucose 112 mg/dL
Small bruise over right hip; ambulates cautiously, steadying against the wall
A: Orthostatic hypotension, likely contributed to by polypharmacy (documented per provider)
Ground-level fall with elevated fall risk; no evidence of acute head or hip injury (per provider)
P: Hold zolpidem; reassess antihypertensive regimen at this visit (per provider)
Order basic metabolic panel; recheck orthostatic vitals at follow-up
Refer to physical therapy for gait and balance training
Provide home-safety handout (remove rugs, night lighting, grab bars) and counsel to rise slowly
Follow up in 2 weeks or sooner if repeat fall or syncope

teaching: Orthostatic hypotension = systolic drop >=20 mmHg or diastolic drop >=10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing; correct technique is supine 5 min, then standing at 1 and 3 minutes.
The MA gathers and documents subjective and objective data - including a full medication reconciliation - but never adjusts, stops, or advises on medications; that is a provider function.
Verify identity with two acceptable identifiers (full name + DOB); room number, appointment time, or answering to a name do not count.
Avoid Do Not Use abbreviations: no trailing zeros (write 5 mg not 5.0 mg), spell out "at bedtime" and "daily" instead of QHS or QD.
scopeNote: The MA obtains orthostatic vitals, reconciles the medication list, and documents the fall history, then routes all diagnosis and medication decisions to the provider.
